But, you know, obviously this is important from the perspective of considerations related to the protocols that we're using the topologies for example but also the security considerations and we'll summarize this entire section focused on the security considerations of north south and east west traffic patterns. So here's what they mean. So first of all, if you use your data center as your compass, for example, let's say this is in the middle here. Any traffic that is entering in or going out of your entire data center is what we consider north-south traffic. If you think about your data center, for example, and you might be connected to the internet, any sort of traffic coming in from the internet or going out to the internet from our own internal network and data center is referred to as north-south traffic and we'll tell you what the difference here between north and south actually is. It'll be pretty easy to understand. Traffic within the data center itself, which could include applications and servers and services, but within the data center itself, is considered to be east-west traffic. So within the data center itself, east-west. Anything that is coming in or going out of the entire data center itself is what we call north-south traffic. I'm going to show you now the inability of my drawing skills, for example. But, you know, let's look at it this way, for example. Let's say this is the big bad internet, for example. And, you know, you've got people out here that obviously want to interface with certain things like your web servers and so on. Here's our data center. We'll call it our data center. And in here we have servers, for example. So any traffic within our own data center within servers, applications, services, network segments, and so on, is obviously going across east-west, for example. So that's what east-west traffic is, any traffic that is happening within the data center itself. And then, of course, if you've got traffic that's either going out from the data center or coming in to the data center from any outside entity or architecture, that's what we call north-south. That makes more sense. This could be obviously machines in here, web browsers, whatever, but it could also be mobile phones, for example, and maybe connected to, let's say, wireless networks or whatever coming in and going out. from the data center itself, this would be considered obviously north-south as well. So east-west, anything within the data center itself. North-south is obviously anything that is either leaving or coming in to the data center itself. Now, we can take a look at this too, but anything that is going this way, So from the data center going out, for example, to something external, you're going up in this case, right? So that is what we call northbound traffic. Northbound traffic is going out of our own data center. up into, for example, the Internet or external connections that you may have. Anything that's coming in from an external connection into the data center is going in this direction. You're going south, for example. Right. So that's the difference. as it relates to north, south, east, west. Okay, so let's just summarize this now. and look at the next slide that we've got here, right? So north-south can basically be summarized as what you see here, right? North-south is any traffic that is either coming in or going out of the data center itself into external architectures. And of course, east-west is any sort of traffic flow applications services servers computers anything within the data center itself right now what's important obviously is to focus on the security aspects of this If you think about this from a traditional perspective, where you have, for example, a network perimeter that protects your internal network and the data center itself, East-West traffic, which is happening within the data center itself, within the network itself, has typically been considered to be trusted. Anything that's happening inside of our network is typically or has been typically considered to be trusted. secure and trusted. North-South traffic, because it's either going out or coming in, ingress and egress is sometimes what the industry refers this as. requires stricter security policies and complete controls that include, for example, firewalls and intrusion detection systems and the ability to be able to react through corrective capabilities and so on. But, you know, this gets a little clouded now because of the advent of zero trust. And you'll remember what zero trust means. It means we trust nothing. We verify everything regardless of where it originates. If we try and apply this concept of zero trust, that obviously is something that companies are moving towards or have moved towards. then this traditional way of looking at things from a north, south, east, west perspective really gets clouded to the point where obviously it doesn't matter Even though traditionally we've trusted east-west traffic more than in zero trust, we can't do that. Because it doesn't matter where something originates and where something terminates, whether it's from outside, of your data center or your network, or inside, we start with zero trust. So it's important to understand this concept that is still being used, right? North, south, east, west, but also how it applies with zero trust. So the key point here is to remember that even though we still use this terminology and traditionally we've trusted east-west traffic more than north-south and north-south traffic has required stricter policies. That's not the case with Zero Trust. In Zero Trust, everything requires stricter policies, regardless of whether it's north-south or east-west. And the way that we address this through zero trust is through more granular security that is focused on really granular things like attributes based parameters that your access controls and so on are able to look at and of course your trust requires real-time constant monitoring and also the ability to be able to react in real time as well.
